does: This paper develops a human-grounded evaluation framework for assessing whether sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on vision models learn interpretable features that correspond to meaningful human concepts. The core contribution is a matching procedure called Fully-Binary Matching Pursuit (FBMP) that maps SAE latents to annotated concepts using a coalition-based approach supporting many-to-one correspondences. To validate interpretability beyond matching metrics, the authors introduce Targeted Attribute Perturbation Alignment Score (TAPAScore), which measures whether matched SAE features respond selectively to targeted image attribute changes. They construct two synthetic benchmarks, synCUB and synCOCO, containing paired images differing in exactly one attribute, enabling controlled evaluation of feature selectivity without user studies. Experiments demonstrate FBMP outperforms simpler one-to-one matching baselines in aligning SAE latents with human concepts.
connects: This work builds on the growing use of sparse autoencoders as mechanistic interpretability tools for understanding vision and vision-language models. It extends prior research on concept-based model interpretability and evaluation of learned representations by moving beyond existing proxy metrics like logistic regression probes or reconstruction accuracy, which often fail to capture semantic alignment. The paper responds to the limitations of qualitative inspection and informal user studies by proposing quantitative, human-grounded measures. It connects to literature on disentangled representations and concept discovery in neural networks, as well as work on targeted perturbation analysis for understanding feature importance. The synthetic benchmark construction draws on intervention-style evaluation paradigms established in causal inference and model interpretability research.
enables: This framework enables more rigorous evaluation of SAE interpretability, supporting future development of better sparse autoencoders by providing quantitative ground truth feedback. The TAPAScore and matching methodology could extend to other representation learning methods and modalities beyond vision. By establishing systematic benchmarks (synCUB, synCOCO) for attribute-based interpretability evaluation, the work opens avenues for creating domain-specific benchmarks in other fields. The coalition-based matching approach generalizes to evaluating many-to-many relationships between model features and concepts, relevant for understanding polysemantic or entangled neurons. This work positions sparse autoencoders as more trustworthy tools for mechanistic interpretability, potentially enabling their use in safety-critical applications where understanding model internals is essential for alignment and robustness verification.
